CEDAR MESA MEDICAL CENTER
Cardiology Clinic - Post-Procedure Follow-up
================================================================

Patient: Deborah Washington                 MRN: CMM-4471-DW
DOB: 1967-09-14                             Age: 58
Sex: Female                                 Race: Black or African American
Ethnicity: Not Hispanic or Latino
Encounter Date: 2026-03-11                  Visit Type: Outpatient, follow-up
Provider: Priya Venkataraman, MD            Service: Cardiology
Referring: Marcus Holloway, MD (Cardiology)

================================================================

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Follow-up after cardiac catheterization for exertional chest
discomfort.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Washington is a 58-year-old woman with a past medical history
of essential hypertension and hyperlipidemia who was referred in
January 2026 for evaluation of exertional chest pressure. Symptoms
first emerged in late fall 2025 with retrosternal tightness on
climbing two flights of stairs, consistently relieved within
3-5 minutes of rest, no associated diaphoresis or radiation.
Stress testing (exercise treadmill with nuclear imaging, 2026-02-04)
was positive for a reversible inferior wall perfusion defect.
She underwent diagnostic left heart catheterization on 2026-02-26
which demonstrated a 75% focal stenosis in the mid right coronary
artery with TIMI 3 flow; the left main, LAD, and circumflex were
angiographically normal. Left ventriculogram showed preserved
systolic function with an estimated ejection fraction of 58%. She
elected optimal medical therapy rather than intervention after
shared decision-making with the interventional team. She returns
today to review the plan and begin anti-anginal therapy.

She reports no chest discomfort at rest. She has had two brief
episodes of pressure over the past ten days, both triggered by
hurrying to catch a bus, relieved promptly with rest. No syncope,
palpitations, orthopnea, or lower extremity edema. She walks
approximately 0.5 miles daily without limitation.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Essential hypertension, diagnosed 2012, well controlled on ACEi
- Hyperlipidemia, diagnosed 2015, on high-intensity statin
- Vitamin D deficiency (resolved with supplementation)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Cholecystectomy, 2003
- Cesarean section, 1998

HOME MEDICATIONS
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily
- Vitamin D3 2000 IU PO daily

ALLERGIES
NKDA.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired school librarian. Never smoker. Alcohol: two glasses of
wine per week. No recreational drug use. Lives with spouse; widowed
sister and adult daughter nearby. Ambulates without assistive
device.

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother with hypertension (alive, age 82). Father deceased age 78
from colon cancer. One sibling, healthy.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: no fevers, chills, weight loss.
Cardiovascular: as per HPI; denies orthopnea, PND, or edema.
Respiratory: no dyspnea at rest, no cough.
GI/GU: unremarkable.
Musculoskeletal: occasional knee stiffness without effusion.
Neurologic: no focal deficits.

VITAL SIGNS
BP 128/74 mmHg   HR 64 bpm   RR 14   Temp 98.2 F   SpO2 98% RA
Weight 72.3 kg   BMI 27.4

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: well-appearing, no acute distress.
HEENT: no JVD, no carotid bruits.
Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, rubs,
or gallops. PMI non-displaced.
Respiratory: clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no bruits.
Extremities: no edema, 2+ distal pulses bilaterally.
Neuro: grossly intact.

DATA REVIEW
ECG (today, in-office): normal sinus rhythm at 62 bpm, normal axis,
no ST-T changes, no Q waves.
Lipid panel (2026-02-10): total cholesterol 162, LDL 84, HDL 54,
triglycerides 118.
HbA1c (2026-02-10): 5.6%.
Catheterization report reviewed as above; no evidence of left main
disease.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stable angina pectoris in the setting of single-vessel coronary
artery disease involving the mid right coronary artery.
   - Initiate carvedilol 6.25 mg PO BID, titrate to target over
     4-6 weeks as tolerated.
   - Continue aspirin 81 mg PO daily.
   - Continue atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily; recheck lipids in
     3 months.
   - Patient educated on recognizing unstable symptoms (rest pain,
     prolonged episodes, associated dyspnea or diaphoresis) and
     instructed to present to the ED for evaluation if these occur.
   - Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg prescribed for breakthrough
     symptoms; use instructions reviewed.

2. Essential hypertension, controlled. Continue lisinopril 20 mg
PO daily. Home BP log reviewed, averaging 126/76.

3. Hyperlipidemia, at goal. Continue atorvastatin.

4. Preventive care: influenza vaccine documented this season;
pneumococcal and shingles vaccinations up to date.

FOLLOW-UP
Return to cardiology clinic in 6 weeks for titration and symptom
review. Urgent return precautions reviewed in writing. Cardiac
rehabilitation referral placed.

================================================================

Credentials: MD, FACC
NPI: 1234567801
Signed: 2026-03-11 14:27

Cardiology Clinic - Heart Failure Follow-up
================================================================

Patient: Gordon Fairweather              MRN: CMM-3329-GF
DOB: 1954-05-18                          Age: 71
Sex: Male                                Race: White
Ethnicity: Not Hispanic or Latino
Encounter Date: 2026-03-26               Visit Type: Outpatient, follow-up
Provider: Marcus Holloway, MD            Service: Cardiology - Heart Failure

================================================================

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Six-month follow-up for heart failure with reduced ejection
fraction.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Fairweather is a 71-year-old retired electrician with HFrEF
of ischemic etiology (diagnosed 2023 after anterior MI with
subsequent revascularization) who returns for routine surveillance.
Initial echocardiogram at diagnosis showed LVEF 30%; serial
imaging has demonstrated reverse remodeling on guideline-directed
medical therapy, with most recent echo (2026-02-18) showing LVEF
40% with mildly dilated LV and no significant valvular disease.

Since his last visit in October 2025 he has been clinically
stable. He walks 30 minutes daily on a treadmill at moderate
incline without symptom-limiting dyspnea. He describes mild
dyspnea on exertion climbing two flights of stairs at a brisk
pace, consistent with NYHA class II functional capacity. No
orthopnea, no PND, no paroxysmal nocturnal cough. He reports
stable weight (daily weights averaging 86 kg, no gains beyond
his 1 kg threshold) and no lower extremity edema. He has not
required any loop diuretic adjustments. No palpitations, syncope,
or near-syncope.

He denies any anginal equivalents and has had no ED visits or
hospitalizations since his last follow-up. He is fully adherent
to his regimen and reports no symptomatic hypotension or
significant side effects.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), ischemic
  etiology, diagnosed 2023
- Prior anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction, 2023, treated
  with PCI to proximal LAD
- Essential hypertension
- Hyperlipidemia
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline eGFR 52)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Percutaneous coronary intervention, 2023
- Right inguinal hernia repair, 2011

HOME MEDICATIONS
- Sacubitril/valsartan 97/103 mg PO BID
- Carvedilol 25 mg PO BID
- Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily
- Dapagliflozin 10 mg PO daily
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily
- Metformin 1000 mg PO BID

ALLERGIES
NKDA.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired electrician. Lives with wife. Former smoker, quit 2018,
15 pack-year history. Alcohol: 1-2 beers per week. Independent
in ADLs and IADLs.

FAMILY HISTORY
Father with coronary disease, CABG at age 68, died age 81 of
unrelated cause. Mother with diabetes and breast cancer, died
age 85.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: no fevers, no weight gain.
Cardiovascular: as per HPI.
Respiratory: no dyspnea at rest, no orthopnea.
Renal: good urinary output.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

VITAL SIGNS
BP 118/72   HR 62   RR 14   Temp 98.1 F   SpO2 97% RA
Weight 86.4 kg   BMI 27.8

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: well-appearing, no acute distress.
Neck: JVP approximately 6 cm above sternal angle (normal).
Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1, soft S2, no S3
or S4, no murmurs. PMI laterally displaced, non-sustained.
Respiratory: clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender; no hepatomegaly.
Extremities: no peripheral edema. 2+ distal pulses.
Neuro: grossly intact.

DATA REVIEW
Echocardiogram (2026-02-18): LVEF 40% (up from 30% at baseline),
mildly dilated LV, no significant valvular disease, no
pericardial effusion.
BMP (2026-03-20): Na 140, K 4.2, BUN 22, Cr 1.4 (eGFR 52, stable),
Cl 102, HCO3 26.
NT-proBNP (2026-03-20): 412 pg/mL (baseline at this visit level
for several months).
HbA1c (2026-03-20): 6.7%.
Lipids (2026-03-20): LDL 62, HDL 48.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, NYHA functional
class II, on four-pillar guideline-directed medical therapy with
stable clinical course and reverse remodeling (LVEF 30% to 40%).
   - Continue sacubitril/valsartan 97/103 mg PO BID.
   - Continue carvedilol 25 mg PO BID.
   - Continue spironolactone 25 mg PO daily.
   - Continue dapagliflozin 10 mg PO daily.
   - Reinforced daily weights, sodium moderation, and symptom
     awareness. Patient has clear action plan for weight gain
     beyond threshold.

2. Chronic kidney disease stage 3a. eGFR stable at 52. Potassium
normal. Continue current regimen.

3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled (HbA1c 6.7%).
Continue metformin. Dapagliflozin providing dual benefit.

4. Coronary artery disease with prior MI, status post PCI to
proximal LAD. Continue aspirin and high-intensity statin.

5. Prior MI as per history; currently no evidence of active
ischemia; no anginal equivalents reported.

FOLLOW-UP
Return to heart failure clinic in 6 months. Labs in 3 months
(BMP, NT-proBNP). No medication changes today. Patient reminded
to obtain annual influenza vaccine.

GREENVALE COMMUNITY HOSPITAL
Emergency Department - Discharge Summary
================================================================

Patient: Miguel Aguirre-Ramos             MRN: GVC-2208-MA
DOB: 1991-07-22                           Age: 34
Sex: Male                                 Race: White
Ethnicity: Hispanic or Latino
Arrival: 2026-01-18 16:42                 Discharge: 2026-01-18 22:10
ESI: 3                                    Disposition: Home
Attending: Samir Bhattacharya, MD         Service: Emergency Medicine

================================================================

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Left lower leg redness, warmth, and pain x 3 days, worsening.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Aguirre-Ramos is a 34-year-old otherwise healthy man who
presents with 3 days of expanding erythema, warmth, and tenderness
over the left anterior shin. Symptoms began as a small red patch
after he scraped his shin on a garden stake while working outdoors
on 2026-01-15. Over the subsequent 48 hours the redness has spread
proximally and distally along the shin and he has developed
low-grade fever (to 38.4 C at home) and mild malaise. He denies
purulent drainage, fluctuance, cold intolerance, joint swelling,
or dyspnea. He took ibuprofen at home with modest relief. No
history of similar infections. No IVDU. No recent travel outside
the region. He works as a landscaper.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Seasonal allergic rhinitis

SURGICAL HISTORY
- None.

MEDICATIONS (home)
- Loratadine 10 mg PO PRN

ALLERGIES
NKDA.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Lives with partner and two children. Works outdoors (landscaper).
Occasional alcohol use (1-2 beers/week). Non-smoker. No illicit
drug use.

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother with type 2 diabetes. Father healthy. Non-contributory.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: low-grade fever, malaise. No chills, no rigors.
Skin: as per HPI.
Cardiovascular: no chest pain, no palpitations.
Respiratory: no cough, no dyspnea.
MSK: no joint pain elsewhere.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

VITAL SIGNS ON ARRIVAL
T 38.2 C   HR 98   BP 132/78   RR 16   SpO2 99% RA   Weight 84 kg

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: well-appearing adult male, non-toxic, alert and oriented.
HEENT: unremarkable.
Cardiac: RRR, no murmurs.
Respiratory: CTAB, no respiratory distress.
Skin/Extremities: left anterior shin with a well-demarcated area
of erythema measuring approximately 12 x 8 cm, warm to touch,
tender on palpation, with mild non-pitting edema. A 1 cm
healing abrasion is present centrally. No fluctuance, no
purulent drainage, no crepitus, no streaking proximally. No
palpable lymphadenopathy in the inguinal region. No signs of
necrotizing fasciitis (pain in excess of exam, bullae, skin
anesthesia, or rapid progression) were noted.
Right lower extremity: unremarkable.

DATA REVIEW
Labs drawn on arrival:
- CBC: WBC 13.2 x 10^9/L with 82% neutrophils. Hgb 14.8. Plt 258.
- BMP within normal limits; lactate 1.6 mmol/L.
- CRP 47 mg/L.
- Blood cultures x 2 drawn prior to antibiotics (pending at
  discharge).

ED COURSE
Patient received IV ceftriaxone 2 g x 1 dose in the ED along with
IV normal saline 1 L for mild hypovolemia. Fever defervesced to
37.6 C after 3 hours. He tolerated oral intake and ambulated
without difficulty. After a period of observation his erythema
showed no proximal progression and he remained hemodynamically
stable.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Cellulitis of left lower limb, likely streptococcal or
staphylococcal origin at an abrasion portal. Non-purulent,
non-severe by IDSA criteria.
   - Discharge on cephalexin 500 mg PO QID x 10 days.
   - Demarcated and photographed at discharge; patient
     instructed to return for expanding erythema beyond the
     demarcation, worsening pain, new fever, streaking, or
     systemic symptoms.
   - Elevate the affected limb when possible; warm compresses.
   - Follow up with primary care within 48-72 hours for clinical
     re-check and review of pending blood cultures.

2. Leukocytosis (WBC 13.2), consistent with acute bacterial
infection; expected to downtrend with treatment.

3. Mild hypovolemia, resolved with IV fluids.

RETURN PRECAUTIONS
Return to ED immediately for: fever > 38.5 C, severe pain, rapid
spread of redness (beyond the demarcation), numbness over the
affected area, blistering or darkening of the skin, drainage of
pus, shortness of breath, confusion, or any symptom the patient
finds concerning.

PORT HALSTON HEALTH SYSTEM
Neurology Clinic - Headache Follow-up
================================================================

Patient: Hana Sato                          MRN: PHH-5812-HS
DOB: 1998-11-04                             Age: 27
Sex: Female                                 Race: Asian
Ethnicity: Not Hispanic or Latino
Encounter Date: 2026-04-02                  Visit Type: Outpatient, follow-up
Provider: Ingrid Lehmann, MD                Service: Neurology - Headache

================================================================

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Four-month follow-up on chronic migraine, reviewing response to
prophylactic therapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Sato is a 27-year-old software engineer with a longstanding
history of migraine without aura, initially diagnosed in her late
teens, who was started on prophylactic topiramate at her last
visit (2025-12-04) after worsening headache frequency over the
prior year. At that visit she reported 16-18 headache days per
month with approximately 8 migraines of disabling severity. She
was started on topiramate 25 mg PO at bedtime with a planned
titration to 50 mg PO BID over 6 weeks.

She returns today having successfully titrated to 50 mg PO BID
by mid-February. Over the past 8 weeks she estimates approximately
5 headache days per month, of which 2 were disabling migraines
requiring abortive therapy (sumatriptan 50 mg PO, taken at home
with good effect). She has tolerated topiramate reasonably well,
with mild paresthesias in the fingertips that have diminished
over time, a decrease in appetite (she has lost 3 kg), and
transient word-finding difficulty which she finds manageable at
work. She denies visual changes, kidney stones, or mood changes.

She uses a headache diary on her phone and reviewed the logs with
me today. Her known triggers remain stress, disrupted sleep, and
skipped meals; she has been more consistent with sleep and meals
over the past 2 months.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Migraine without aura, chronic, diagnosed 2017
- Iron deficiency anemia (2022, resolved with supplementation)
- Acne vulgaris, mild

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Tonsillectomy, age 8

HOME MEDICATIONS
- Topiramate 50 mg PO BID (prophylaxis)
- Sumatriptan 50 mg PO PRN for acute migraine (max 2 doses in
  24 hours, no more than 10 days per month)
- Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO daily (maintenance)

ALLERGIES
NKDA.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Lives with partner. Works from home as a software engineer.
Non-smoker. Alcohol: rare social use. No recreational drug use.
Exercises 3x/week (yoga).

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother with migraine. Father healthy. One younger sister, healthy.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Neurologic: as per HPI, intermittent fingertip paresthesias.
Psych: no depression, no anxiety.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

VITAL SIGNS
BP 112/68   HR 72   RR 14   Temp 98.0 F   SpO2 99% RA
Weight 58.1 kg   BMI 21.2

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: alert, no distress.
Cranial nerves II-XII intact including fundoscopy (discs sharp,
no papilledema).
Motor: 5/5 throughout, normal tone.
Sensory: symmetric to light touch and pinprick.
Reflexes: 2+ symmetric, plantars downgoing.
Coordination: finger-nose-finger and heel-knee-shin normal.
Gait: steady with normal base and arm swing.

DATA REVIEW
Prior brain MRI (2024-06-12): unremarkable, no demyelinating
lesions, no mass, no chronic small vessel disease.
Headache diary reviewed today.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Chronic migraine without aura, improved on topiramate at 100 mg
total daily dose. Monthly headache days decreased from ~17 to ~5,
disabling migraines from ~8 to ~2, consistent with clinically
meaningful response.
   - Continue topiramate 50 mg PO BID.
   - Continue sumatriptan PRN for acute migraine with current
     medication-overuse limits reviewed.
   - Reinforced lifestyle counseling: sleep consistency, meal
     timing, hydration, caffeine moderation.
   - Discussed CGRP monoclonal antibody options (e.g., erenumab)
     as a future step if response plateaus or tolerability
     worsens.

2. Topiramate tolerability.
   - Paresthesias mild and improving; continue to monitor.
   - Weight change noted; discussed ensuring adequate caloric
     intake. Will recheck weight next visit.

3. Iron deficiency anemia, resolved.
   - Continue maintenance ferrous sulfate; last ferritin at goal.

FOLLOW-UP
Return to clinic in 4 months. Headache diary to continue. Patient
to contact clinic sooner for: new focal neurologic symptoms,
escalation of migraine frequency, medication intolerance, or
concern for medication-overuse headache.

================================================================

48M p/w three complaints per pt: "chest pain, swollen leg, and
a bad headache" all present today. CP described as fleeting
sharp left sided, no exertional component, no radiation, no
diaphoresis, better when distracted. HA described as
band-like bifrontal pressure, 5/10, ongoing most of day, relieved
partially by ibuprofen. R leg swelling x 1 wk, has hx of similar
prior episodes "the vein doctor said it was chronic." No acute
change today, no redness, no calf pain.

PMH: HTN, obesity, chronic venous insufficiency (R leg, dx 2020
by vascular), anxiety disorder. No prior CAD or DVT per chart.
Meds: lisinopril 20 qd, sertraline 50 qd. NKDA. SH: current
smoker 15 pk-yr, EtOH 3-4 beers/week, denies IVDU.

Exam: alert, appears comfortable. VS: 138/86, HR 82, RR 16, 98%
RA, afeb. CV RRR no m. Lungs clear. Abd soft. R leg w/ mild
pitting edema ankle->mid calf, hyperpigmentation along medial
gaiter area, 2+ DP pulses, no cords, no calf tenderness, Homan's
neg (acknowledged low sens). Neuro grossly intact, no focal
deficit, cranial nerves intact, no photophobia, no meningismus.

Workup: EKG - NSR 78, no acute STT changes. Trop neg x1. D-dimer
sent given leg swelling despite chronic hx - returned 380 (wnl).
CBC/BMP unremarkable. UDS not performed.

ED course: observed approx 3 hrs, pain episodes did not recur.
Reassured.

A/P:
1. Chest pain, non-cardiac, non-pleuritic, no red flags. No
evidence of ACS - EKG nl, trop neg. Likely musculoskeletal vs
anxiety-related, pt agrees sxs are similar to prior non-cardiac
episodes. R07.9.
2. Chronic venous insufficiency of the right lower extremity,
stable, consistent w/ prior dx. No signs of DVT, D-dimer negative.
Continue compression stockings, elevation. PCP f/u.
3. Tension-type headache. No red flags. OTC analgesics as needed.
Sleep hygiene and hydration counseled.

Counseled re: smoking cessation, offered nicotine patch
prescription, pt declined today but will consider. Return
precautions reviewed. Discharged home.
